cells.epoch_label: Chaos of primitive expression-forms. Mystical symbolism and naïve imitation.
cells.text: Thinite Period (3400–3000).

cells.epoch_label: Chaos of primitive expression-forms. Mystical symbolism and naïve imitation.
cells.text: Mycenean Age (1600–1700). Fore-cultures: Late-Egyptian (Minoan), Late-Babylonian (Asia Minor).
cells.note: “(1600–1700)” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344) — inconsistent with Table III, which dates the Mycenean Age 1600–1100 (the historical range); transcribed as printed.

cells.epoch_label: Chaos of primitive expression-forms. Mystical symbolism and naïve imitation.
cells.text: Persian-Seleucid Period (500–0). Fore-cultures: Late-Classical (Hellenistic), Late-Indian (Indo-Iranian).

cells.epoch_label: Chaos of primitive expression-forms. Mystical symbolism and naïve imitation.
cells.text: Merovingian-Carolingian Era (500–900).

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — ornament and architecture as the elementary expression of the young world-feeling (the “Primitives”).
cells.text: Old Kingdom (2900–2400).

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — ornament and architecture as the elementary expression of the young world-feeling (the “Primitives”).
Early Period — ornament and architecture as the elementary expression of the young world-feeling (the “Primitives”).
cells.text: Early-Arabian form-world (0–500): Sassanid, Byzantine, Armenian, Syrian, Sabæan, “Late-Classical” and “Early Christian”.

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — ornament and architecture as the elementary expression of the young world-feeling (the “Primitives”).
Birth and rise. Forms sprung from the land, unconsciously shaped.
cells.text: Dynasties IV–V (2930–2625). Geometrical temple-style. Pyramid temples. Ranked plant-columns. Rows of flat-relief. Tomb statues.

cells.epoch_label: Birth and rise. Forms sprung from the land, unconsciously shaped.
cells.text: 11th to 9th Centuries. Timber building. Doric column. Architrave. Geometric (Dipylon) style. Burial urns.

cells.epoch_label: Birth and rise. Forms sprung from the land, unconsciously shaped.
cells.text: 1st to 3rd Centuries. Cult interiors. Basilica, cupola (Pantheon as mosque). Column-and-arch. Stem-tracery filling blanks. Sarcophagus.

cells.epoch_label: Birth and rise. Forms sprung from the land, unconsciously shaped.
cells.text: 11th to 13th Centuries. Romanesque and Early-Gothic vaulted cathedrals. Flying buttress. Glass-painting; cathedral sculpture.

cells.epoch_label: Completion of the early form-language. Exhaustion of possibilities. Contradiction.
cells.text: VI Dynasty (2625–2574). Extinction of the pyramid-style and the epic-idyllic relief style. Floraison of archaic portrait-plastic painting.

cells.epoch_label: Completion of the early form-language. Exhaustion of possibilities. Contradiction.
cells.text: 8th and 7th Centuries. End of the archaic Doric-Etruscan style. Proto-Corinthian–Early-Attic (mythological) vase.

cells.epoch_label: Completion of the early form-language. Exhaustion of possibilities. Contradiction.
cells.text: 4th–5th Centuries. End of Syrian, Persian and Coptic pictorial art. Rise of mosaic-picturing and of arabesque.

cells.epoch_label: Completion of the early form-language. Exhaustion of possibilities. Contradiction.
cells.text: 14th–15th Centuries. Late Gothic and Renaissance. Floraison and waning of fresco and statue, from Giotto (Gothic) to Michelangelo (Baroque); Siena, Nürnberg; the Gothic picture from Van Eyck to Holbein. Counterpoint and oil-painting.

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — a group of urban, conscious arts in the hands of individuals (the “Great Masters”).
cells.text: Middle Kingdom (2150–1800).

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — a group of urban, conscious arts in the hands of individuals (the “Great Masters”).
Late Period — a group of urban, conscious arts in the hands of individuals (the “Great Masters”).
cells.text: Late-Arabian form-world (500–800): Persian-Nestorian, Byzantine-Armenian, Islamic-Moorish.

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — a group of urban, conscious arts in the hands of individuals (the “Great Masters”).
Formation of a mature artistry.
cells.text: XIth Dynasty. Delicate and telling art (almost no traces left).

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a mature artistry.
cells.text: Completion of the temple-body (peripteros, in stone). The Ionic column. Reign of fresco-painting till Polygnotus (460). Rise of free plastic “in the round” (“Apollo of Tenea” to Hageladas).

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a mature artistry.
cells.text: Completion of the mosque-interior (central dome of Hagia Sophia). Zenith of mosaic painting. Completion of the carpet-like arabesque style (Machatta).

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a mature artistry.
cells.text: The pictorial style in architecture, from Michelangelo to Bernini (d. 1680). Reign of oil-painting from Titian to Rembrandt (d. 1664). Rise of music from Orlando Lasso to H. Schütz (d. 1671).

cells.epoch_label: Perfection of an intellectualized form-language.
cells.text: XIIth Dynasty (2000–1788). Pylon-temple, labyrinth. Character-statuary and historical reliefs.

cells.epoch_label: Perfection of an intellectualized form-language.
cells.text: Maturity of Athens (480–350). The Acropolis. Reign of Classical plastic from Myron to Phidias. End of strict fresco and ceramic painting (Zeuxis).

cells.epoch_label: Perfection of an intellectualized form-language.
cells.text: Ommayads (7th–8th Century). Complete victory of the featureless arabesque over architecture also.

cells.epoch_label: Perfection of an intellectualized form-language.
cells.text: Rococo. Musical architecture (“Rococo”). Reign of Classical music from Bach to Mozart. End of Classical oil-painting (Watteau to Goya).

cells.epoch_label: Exhaustion of strict creativeness. Dissolution of the grand form. End of the Style: “Classicism” and “Romanticism”.
cells.text: Confusion after about 1750 (no remains).

cells.epoch_label: Exhaustion of strict creativeness. Dissolution of the grand form. End of the Style: “Classicism” and “Romanticism”.
cells.text: The age of Alexander. The Corinthian column. Lysippus and Apelles.

cells.epoch_label: Exhaustion of strict creativeness. Dissolution of the grand form. End of the Style: “Classicism” and “Romanticism”.
cells.text: “Haroun-al-Raschid” (about 800). “Moorish art”.

cells.epoch_label: Exhaustion of strict creativeness. Dissolution of the grand form. End of the Style: “Classicism” and “Romanticism”.
cells.text: Empire and Biedermeyer. Classicist taste in architecture. Beethoven, Delacroix.

cells.epoch_label: “Modern art.” “Art-problems.” Attempts to portray or excite the megalopolitan consciousness; music, architecture and painting turn into mere craft-arts.
cells.text: Hyksos Period (preserved only in Crete; Minoan art).

cells.epoch_label: “Modern art.” “Art-problems.” Attempts to portray or excite the megalopolitan consciousness; music, architecture and painting turn into mere craft-arts.
cells.text: Hellenism. Pergamene art (theatricality). Hellenistic painting modes (veristic, bizarre, subjective). Architectural display in the cities of the Diadochi.

cells.epoch_label: “Modern art.” “Art-problems.” Attempts to portray or excite the megalopolitan consciousness; music, architecture and painting turn into mere craft-arts.
cells.text: Sultan dynasties of the 9th–10th Century. Prime of Spanish-Sicilian art. Samarra.

cells.epoch_label: “Modern art.” “Art-problems.” Attempts to portray or excite the megalopolitan consciousness; music, architecture and painting turn into mere craft-arts.
cells.text: 19th and 20th Centuries. Liszt, Berlioz, Wagner. Impressionism from Constable to Leibl and Manet. American architecture.

cells.epoch_label: End of form-development. Meaningless, empty, pretentious architecture and ornament. Imitation of archaic and exotic motives.
cells.text: XVIII Dynasty (1580–1350). Rock-temple of Dehr-el-Bahri. Memnon-colossi. Art of Cnossos and Amarna.

cells.epoch_label: End of form-development. Meaningless, empty, pretentious architecture and ornament. Imitation of archaic and exotic motives.
cells.text: Roman Period (100–0–100). Indiscriminate piling of all three orders. Fora, theatres (Colosseum). Triumphal arches.

cells.epoch_label: End of form-development. Meaningless, empty, pretentious architecture and ornament. Imitation of archaic and exotic motives.
cells.text: Seljuks (from 1050). “Oriental art” of the Crusade period.

cells.epoch_label: End of form-development. Meaningless, empty, pretentious architecture and ornament. Imitation of archaic and exotic motives.
cells.text: From 2000.
cells.note: Spengler leaves the Western cell as a forward projection — this Civilization-phase lies in the West's future.

cells.epoch_label: Finale. A fixed stock of forms. Imperial display by means of material and mass. Provincial craft-art.
cells.text: XIX Dynasty (1350–1205). Gigantic buildings of Luxor, Karnak and Abydos. Small-art (beast-plastic, textiles, arms).

cells.epoch_label: Finale. A fixed stock of forms. Imperial display by means of material and mass. Provincial craft-art.
cells.text: Trajan to Aurelian. Gigantic fora, thermæ, colonnades, triumphal arches. Roman provincial art (ceramic, statuary, arms).

cells.epoch_label: Finale. A fixed stock of forms. Imperial display by means of material and mass. Provincial craft-art.
cells.text: Mongol Period (from 1250). Gigantic buildings (e.g. in India). Oriental craft-art (rugs, arms, implements).

cells.epoch_label: Finale. A fixed stock of forms. Imperial display by means of material and mass. Provincial craft-art.
cells.text: From 2000.
cells.note: As above, Spengler leaves the Western cell as a forward projection.

cells.epoch_label: Primitive folk. Tribes and their chiefs. As yet no “politics” and no “state”.
cells.text: Thinite Period (Menes), 3400–3000.

cells.epoch_label: Primitive folk. Tribes and their chiefs. As yet no “politics” and no “state”.
cells.text: Mycenean Age (“Agamemnon”), 1600–1100.

cells.epoch_label: Primitive folk. Tribes and their chiefs. As yet no “politics” and no “state”.
cells.text: Shang Period (1700–1300).

cells.epoch_label: Primitive folk. Tribes and their chiefs. As yet no “politics” and no “state”.
cells.text: Frankish Period (Charlemagne), 500–900.

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — organic articulation of political existence; the two prime classes (noble and priest); feudal economics, purely agrarian values.
cells.text: Old Kingdom (2900–2400).

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — organic articulation of political existence; the two prime classes (noble and priest); feudal economics, purely agrarian values.
cells.text: Doric Period (1100–650).

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — organic articulation of political existence; the two prime classes (noble and priest); feudal economics, purely agrarian values.
cells.text: Early Chou Period (1300–800).

cells.epoch_label: Early Period — organic articulation of political existence; the two prime classes (noble and priest); feudal economics, purely agrarian values.
cells.text: Gothic Period (900–1500).

cells.epoch_label: Feudalism. Spirit of countryside and countryman; the “City” only a market or stronghold; chivalric-religious ideals; struggles of vassals among themselves and against the overlord.
cells.text: Feudal conditions of the IV Dynasty. Increasing power of the feudatories and priesthoods. The Pharaoh as incarnation of Ra.

cells.epoch_label: Feudalism. Spirit of countryside and countryman; the “City” only a market or stronghold; chivalric-religious ideals; struggles of vassals among themselves and against the overlord.
cells.text: The Homeric kingship. Rise of the nobility (Ithaca, Etruria, Sparta).

cells.epoch_label: Feudalism. Spirit of countryside and countryman; the “City” only a market or stronghold; chivalric-religious ideals; struggles of vassals among themselves and against the overlord.
cells.text: The central ruler (Wang) pressed hard by the feudal nobility.

cells.epoch_label: Feudalism. Spirit of countryside and countryman; the “City” only a market or stronghold; chivalric-religious ideals; struggles of vassals among themselves and against the overlord.
cells.text: Roman-German Imperial period. Crusading nobility. Empire and Papacy.

cells.epoch_label: Crisis and dissolution of patriarchal forms. From feudalism to the aristocratic State.
cells.text: VI Dynasty: break-up of the Kingdom into heritable principalities. VII and VIII Dynasties: interregnum.

cells.epoch_label: Crisis and dissolution of patriarchal forms. From feudalism to the aristocratic State.
cells.text: Aristocratic synoecism. Dissolution of kinship into annual offices. Oligarchy.

cells.epoch_label: Crisis and dissolution of patriarchal forms. From feudalism to the aristocratic State.
cells.text: 934–904: I-Wang and the vassals. 842: interregnum.

cells.epoch_label: Crisis and dissolution of patriarchal forms. From feudalism to the aristocratic State.
cells.text: Territorial princes. Renaissance towns. Lancaster and York. 1254: interregnum.

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — the matured State-idea actualized; town versus countryside; rise of the Third Estate (bourgeoisie); victory of money over landed property.
cells.text: Middle Kingdom (2150–1800).

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — the matured State-idea actualized; town versus countryside; rise of the Third Estate (bourgeoisie); victory of money over landed property.
cells.text: Ionic Period (650–300).

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — the matured State-idea actualized; town versus countryside; rise of the Third Estate (bourgeoisie); victory of money over landed property.
cells.text: Late Chou Period (800–500).

cells.epoch_label: Late Period — the matured State-idea actualized; town versus countryside; rise of the Third Estate (bourgeoisie); victory of money over landed property.
cells.text: Baroque Period (1500–1800).

cells.epoch_label: Fashioning of a world of States of strict form. Frondes.
cells.text: XIth Dynasty: overthrow of the baronage by the rulers of Thebes. Centralized bureaucracy-state.

cells.epoch_label: Fashioning of a world of States of strict form. Frondes.
cells.text: 6th Century: First Tyrannis (Cleisthenes, Periander, Polycrates, the Tarquins). The City-State.

cells.epoch_label: Fashioning of a world of States of strict form. Frondes.
cells.text: Period of the “Protectors” (Ming-Chu, 685–591) and the rulers of Thebes; congresses of princes (to 460).
cells.note: “and the rulers of Thebes” appears in this cell in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344); it also appears in the Egyptian column, and is most likely an error in the printed table, but it is transcribed here as printed.

cells.epoch_label: Fashioning of a world of States of strict form. Frondes.
cells.text: Dynastic family-power; the Fronde (Richelieu, Wallenstein, Cromwell), about 1630.

cells.epoch_label: Climax of the State-form (“Absolutism”). Unity of town and “Society”; the “three estates”.
cells.text: XIIth Dynasty (2000–1788). Strictest centralization of power. Court and finance nobility.

cells.epoch_label: Climax of the State-form (“Absolutism”). Unity of town and “Society”; the “three estates”.
cells.text: The pure Polis (absolutism of the Demos). Agora politics. Rise of the tribunate. Themistocles, Pericles.

cells.epoch_label: Climax of the State-form (“Absolutism”). Unity of town and “Society”; the “three estates”.
cells.text: Chun-Chiu period (“Spring and Autumn”), 590–480. Seven powers. Perfection of social forms (Li).

cells.epoch_label: Climax of the State-form (“Absolutism”). Unity of town and “Society”; the “three estates”.
cells.text: Ancien Régime. Rococo. Court nobility of Versailles. Cabinet politics. Habsburg and Bourbon. Louis XIV. Frederick the Great.

cells.epoch_label: Break-up of the State-form (Revolution and Napoleonism). Victory of the city over the countryside, of the “people” over the privileged, of money over policy.
cells.text: 1788–1680: revolution and military government. Decay of the realm; small potentates, in some cases sprung from the people.

cells.epoch_label: Break-up of the State-form (Revolution and Napoleonism). Victory of the city over the countryside, of the “people” over the privileged, of money over policy.
cells.text: 4th Century: social revolution and Second Tyrannis (Dionysius I, Jason of Pherae, Appius Claudius the Censor). Alexander. Revolutions and annihilation-wars.

cells.epoch_label: Break-up of the State-form (Revolution and Napoleonism). Victory of the city over the countryside, of the “people” over the privileged, of money over policy.
cells.text: 480: beginning of the Chan-Kwo period. 441: fall of the Chou dynasty.

cells.epoch_label: Break-up of the State-form (Revolution and Napoleonism). Victory of the city over the countryside, of the “people” over the privileged, of money over policy.
cells.text: End of the 18th Century: revolution in America and France (Washington, Fox, Mirabeau, Robespierre). Napoleon.

cells.epoch_label: Domination of money (“Democracy”). Economic powers permeating the political forms and authorities.
cells.text: 1680 (1788)–1580: Hyksos period; deepest decline; dictatures of alien generals (Chian). 288: the imperial title. After 1600: definitive victory of the rulers of Thebes.

cells.epoch_label: Domination of money (“Democracy”). Economic powers permeating the political forms and authorities.
cells.text: 300–100: political Hellenism. From Alexander to Hannibal and Scipio, royal all-power; from Cleomenes III and C. Flaminius (220) to C. Marius, radical demagogues.

cells.epoch_label: Domination of money (“Democracy”). Economic powers permeating the political forms and authorities.
cells.text: 480–230: the period of the “Contending States”. The imperialist statesmen of Tsin. From 289: incorporation of the last states in the Empire.

cells.epoch_label: Domination of money (“Democracy”). Economic powers permeating the political forms and authorities.
cells.text: 1800–2000: the 19th Century, from Napoleon to the World-War — the “system of the Great Powers”, standing armies, constitutions; the 20th-Century transition from constitutional to informal sway of individuals; annihilation-wars; imperialism.

cells.epoch_label: Formation of Cæsarism. Victory of force-politics over money; the nations sink into a formless population, ruled as an imperium of growing crudity.
cells.text: 1580–1350: XVIIIth Dynasty. Thuthmosis III.

cells.epoch_label: Formation of Cæsarism. Victory of force-politics over money; the nations sink into a formless population, ruled as an imperium of growing crudity.
cells.text: 100–0–100: Sulla to Domitian. Cæsar, Tiberius.

cells.epoch_label: Formation of Cæsarism. Victory of force-politics over money; the nations sink into a formless population, ruled as an imperium of growing crudity.
cells.text: 250–0–26: the House of Wang-Cheng and the Western Han Dynasty. 221: the Augustus-title (Shi) of the Emperor Hwang-Ti. 140–80: Wu-ti.

cells.epoch_label: Formation of Cæsarism. Victory of force-politics over money; the nations sink into a formless population, ruled as an imperium of growing crudity.
cells.note: “1000–1200” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344). The value is anomalous — the West's Cæsarism is elsewhere a forward projection (cf. the preceding “1800–2000”) — but it is what the table prints.

cells.epoch_label: Maturing of the final form. Private and family policies of individual leaders; the world as spoil; Egypticism, Mandarinism, Byzantinism; ossification even of the imperial machinery.
cells.text: 1350–1205: XIXth Dynasty. Sethos I. Rameses II.

cells.epoch_label: Maturing of the final form. Private and family policies of individual leaders; the world as spoil; Egypticism, Mandarinism, Byzantinism; ossification even of the imperial machinery.
cells.text: 100–300: Trajan to Aurelian. Trajan, Septimius Severus.

cells.epoch_label: Maturing of the final form. Private and family policies of individual leaders; the world as spoil; Egypticism, Mandarinism, Byzantinism; ossification even of the imperial machinery.
cells.text: 25–220 A.D.: Eastern Han Dynasty. 58–71: Ming-ti.

cells.epoch_label: Maturing of the final form. Private and family policies of individual leaders; the world as spoil; Egypticism, Mandarinism, Byzantinism; ossification even of the imperial machinery.
cells.text: after 1200.
cells.note: “after 1200” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344); anomalous for the same reason as the preceding row, but transcribed as printed.

cells.epoch_label: Birth of a myth of the grand style, expressing a new God-feeling. World-fear and world-longing.
cells.text: Vedic religion. Aryan hero-tales.

cells.epoch_label: Birth of a myth of the grand style, expressing a new God-feeling. World-fear and world-longing.
cells.text: Hellenic-Italian “Demeter” religion of the people. Homer. Heracles and Theseus legends.

cells.epoch_label: Birth of a myth of the grand style, expressing a new God-feeling. World-fear and world-longing.
cells.text: Primitive Christianity (Mandaeans, Marcion, Gnosis, Syncretism: Mithras, Baal). Gospels, Apocalypses. Christian, Mazdaist and pagan legends.

cells.epoch_label: Birth of a myth of the grand style, expressing a new God-feeling. World-fear and world-longing.
cells.text: German Catholicism. Edda (Baldr). Bernard of Clairvaux, Joachim of Floris, Francis of Assisi. Popular Epos (Siegfried). Western legends of the Saints.

cells.epoch_label: Earliest mystical-metaphysical shaping of the new world-outlook. Zenith of Scholasticism.
cells.text: Preserved in the oldest parts of the Vedas.

cells.epoch_label: Earliest mystical-metaphysical shaping of the new world-outlook. Zenith of Scholasticism.
cells.text: Oldest (oral) Orphic, Etruscan discipline. After-effect: Hesiod, Cosmogonies.

cells.epoch_label: Earliest mystical-metaphysical shaping of the new world-outlook. Zenith of Scholasticism.
cells.text: Origen (d. 254), Plotinus (d. 269), Mani (d. 276), Iamblichus (d. 330). Avesta, Talmud, Patristic literature.

cells.epoch_label: Earliest mystical-metaphysical shaping of the new world-outlook. Zenith of Scholasticism.
cells.text: Thomas Aquinas (d. 1274), Duns Scotus (d. 1308), Dante (d. 1321), Eckhardt (d. 1329). Mysticism. Scholasticism.

cells.epoch_label: Reformation: internal popular opposition to the great springtime forms.
cells.text: Brahmanas. Oldest parts of the Upanishads (10th and 9th Centuries).

cells.epoch_label: Reformation: internal popular opposition to the great springtime forms.
cells.text: Orphic movement. Dionysiac religion. “Numa” religion (7th Century).

cells.epoch_label: Reformation: internal popular opposition to the great springtime forms.
cells.text: Augustine (d. 430). Nestorians (c. 430). Monophysites (c. 450). Mazdak (c. 500).

cells.epoch_label: Reformation: internal popular opposition to the great springtime forms.
cells.text: Nicolaus Cusanus (d. 1464). John Hus (d. 1415). Savonarola, Karlstadt, Luther, Calvin (d. 1564).
cells.note: The source OCR misprints Hus's death as 1308; he was burned at Constance in 1415.

cells.epoch_label: Beginning of a purely philosophical form of the world-feeling. Opposition of idealistic and realistic systems.
cells.text: Preserved in the Upanishads.

cells.epoch_label: Beginning of a purely philosophical form of the world-feeling. Opposition of idealistic and realistic systems.
cells.text: The great Pre-Socratics (6th and 5th Centuries).

cells.epoch_label: Beginning of a purely philosophical form of the world-feeling. Opposition of idealistic and realistic systems.
cells.text: Byzantine, Jewish, Syrian, Coptic and Persian literature of the 6th and 7th Centuries.

cells.epoch_label: Beginning of a purely philosophical form of the world-feeling. Opposition of idealistic and realistic systems.
cells.text: Galileo, Bacon, Descartes, Bruno, Boehme, Leibniz (16th and 17th Centuries).

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a new mathematic. Conception of number as copy and content of world-form.
cells.text: (lost)

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a new mathematic. Conception of number as copy and content of world-form.
cells.text: Number as magnitude (proportion). Geometry, Arithmetic. Pythagoreans (from 540).

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a new mathematic. Conception of number as copy and content of world-form.
cells.text: The indefinite number (Algebra). (Development not yet investigated.)

cells.epoch_label: Formation of a new mathematic. Conception of number as copy and content of world-form.
cells.text: Number as Function (analysis). Descartes, Pascal, Fermat (c. 1630). Newton and Leibniz (c. 1670).

cells.epoch_label: Puritanism: rationalistic-mystic impoverishment of religion.
cells.text: (lost)

cells.epoch_label: Puritanism: rationalistic-mystic impoverishment of religion.
cells.text: Pythagorean society (from 540).

cells.epoch_label: Puritanism: rationalistic-mystic impoverishment of religion.
cells.text: Mohammed (622). Paulicians and Iconoclasts (from 650).

cells.epoch_label: Puritanism: rationalistic-mystic impoverishment of religion.
cells.text: English Puritans (from 1620). French Jansenists (from 1640), Port Royal.

cells.epoch_label: “Enlightenment.” Belief in the almightiness of reason. Cult of “Nature.” “Rational” religion.
cells.text: Sutras; Sankhya; Buddha; later Upanishads.

cells.epoch_label: “Enlightenment.” Belief in the almightiness of reason. Cult of “Nature.” “Rational” religion.
cells.text: Sophists of the 5th Century. Socrates (d. 399). Democritus (d. c. 360).

cells.epoch_label: “Enlightenment.” Belief in the almightiness of reason. Cult of “Nature.” “Rational” religion.
cells.text: Mutazilites. Sufism. Nazzam, Alkindi (c. 830).

cells.epoch_label: “Enlightenment.” Belief in the almightiness of reason. Cult of “Nature.” “Rational” religion.
cells.text: English Rationalists (Locke). French Encyclopaedists (Voltaire). Rousseau.

cells.epoch_label: Zenith of mathematical thought. Elucidation of the form-world of numbers.
cells.text: (lost)

cells.epoch_label: Zenith of mathematical thought. Elucidation of the form-world of numbers.
cells.text: Archytas (d. 365). Plato (d. 346). (Conic Sections.)

cells.epoch_label: Zenith of mathematical thought. Elucidation of the form-world of numbers.
cells.text: (Not investigated.) (Theory of number. Spherical Trigonometry.)

cells.epoch_label: Zenith of mathematical thought. Elucidation of the form-world of numbers.
cells.text: Euler (d. 1763), Lagrange (d. 1813), Laplace (d. 1827). (The Infinitesimal problem.)

cells.epoch_label: The great conclusive systems.
cells.text: Idealism (Yoga, Vedanta). Epistemology (Vaisheshika). Logic (Nyaya).

cells.epoch_label: The great conclusive systems.
cells.text: Plato (d. 346). Aristotle (d. 322).

cells.epoch_label: The great conclusive systems.
cells.text: Alfarabi (d. 950). Avicenna (d. c. 1000).

cells.epoch_label: The great conclusive systems.
cells.text: Goethe. Kant. Schelling, Hegel, Fichte.

cells.epoch_label: Materialistic world-outlook: cult of science, utility and prosperity.
cells.text: Sankhya. Tscharvaka (Lokoyata).

cells.epoch_label: Materialistic world-outlook: cult of science, utility and prosperity.
cells.text: Cynics, Cyrenaics. Last Sophists (Pyrrhon).

cells.epoch_label: Materialistic world-outlook: cult of science, utility and prosperity.
cells.text: Communistic, atheistic, Epicurean sects of Abbassid times. “Brethren of Sincerity.”

cells.epoch_label: Materialistic world-outlook: cult of science, utility and prosperity.
cells.text: Bentham, Comte, Darwin. Spencer, Stirner, Marx, Feuerbach.

cells.epoch_label: Ethical-social ideals of life. Epoch of “unmathematical philosophy.” Skepsis.
